California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
stephen D. Schuett 
Assistant County Counsel 
County of Kern 
October 10, 1989 
Administration and Courts Building 
1415 Truxtun Avenue, 5th Floor 
Bakersfield, CA 
93301 
Dear Mr. Schuett: 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-89-545 
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on 
behalf of Kern County Sheriff John R. smith concerning the ap-
plication of the mass mailing provisions of the Political Reform 
Act (the "Act") to the "Crime Prevention News,ii a newsletter 
produced and distributed by the Kern County Sheriff's Depart-
ment. 1/ 
Please note that the Commission is in the process of revising 
Regulation 18901, which implements the statutory mass mailing 
restrictions. 
We anticipate noticing amendments to the regulation 
in October 1989, and adopting those amendments at the December 13, 
1989 Commission meeting. 
A draft of the proposed amendments is 
enclosed. 
Nevertheless, until these amendments are formally 
adopted by the Commission, the current version of Regulation 18901 
remains in force. 
Our analysis in this letter is based upon the 
current version of this regulation. 
QUESTIONS 
1. 
Is a newsletter produced by the Kern County Sheriff's 
Department which contains a message from the elected sheriff a 
prohibited mass mailing under the Act? 
2. 
Are copies of the Kern County Sheriff's Department 
newsletter distributed at neighborhood watch meetings or the 
1 
Government Code Section 81000-91015. 
All statutory references 
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Commission 
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations section 
18000, 
~ 
All references to regulations are to Title 2, 
D 
on 
of 
Cali 
Code of 
ons. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • 
P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804~0807 • (916) 322-5660 


File No. A-89-545 
Page 2 
county fair, or mailed out to persons that sign up at these loca-
tions, distributed in response to unsolicited specific requests? 
CONCLUSIONS 
1. 
Where the sheriff authors a column in the newsletter, 
exercises direction and control over the content of the column, 
and is designated as the author in the byline of the column, there 
is sufficient direction and control over the newsletter to make it 
a prohibited mass mailing under the Act. 
2. 
The Kern County Sheriff's Department newsletter may be 
placed at the sheriff's department's, neighborhood watch meetings 
and in booths at the local county fair. 
Such mailings are 
distributed in response to an unsolicited specific request and are 
exempted from the mass mailing restrictions of the Act provided 
the request for the materials is not induced by sheriff's depart-
ment personnel. 
Similarly, copies mailed to persons that sign-up 
for the Sheriff's Department's mailing list at these locations are 
also exempted if the request for the materials is not induced by 
department personnel. 
FACTS 
The Kern County Sheriff's Department (the "department") 
produces a newsletter titled the "Crime Prevention News." 
Each 
month more than 200 copies of the newsletter are distributed, 
primarily to persons on the department's mailing list. 
Persons 
interested in receiving the newsletter and other department mail-
ings may sign up on the mailing list at the department's neighbor-
hood watch meetings. 
Printed at the top of the mailing list sign-
up sheets is the following: 
"I request the Kern County Sheriff's 
Dept. send me information regarding Crime Prevention and other 
areas of law enforcement," or similar language. 
In addition, copies of the newsletter are distributed at the 
depqrtment's neighborhood watch meetings. 
The department person-
nel that conduct the meetings set out copies of the newsletter, 
along with the mailing list sign-up sheet. 
Department personnel 
will normally make reference to the sign-up sheet and the avail-
ability of the newsletters in their presentations. 
The department is also planning to distribute newsletters and 
other material in the same manner from a booth at the local county 
fair. 
They will also provide a sign-up sheet at the booth for 
those who wish to be added to the mailing list and, additionally, 
to those persons interested in becoming deputies. 
ANALYSIS 
Prohibited Mass Mailings 
Section 
9001 
that no newsletter or other mass mail~ 
ing shall be sent at public expense. 
Section 82041.5 defines a 


File No. A-89-545 
Page 3 
"mass mailing" as more than 200 substantially similar pieces of 
mail sent in a calendar month. 
Regulation 18901(c) (copy 
enclosed) provides that a newsletter or other mass mailing may not 
be sent within the meaning of section 89001 if: 
(1) 
The name of the elected officer or 
his or her photograph appears on the document; 
and 
(A) 
The elected officer exercises 
direction or control over the content, 
production, or distribution of the 
document, or 
(B) 
The document is sent at the 
request or suggestion of the elected of-
ficer or his or her agent; or 
(C) 
The document is signed by, or 
is designated as being from, the elected 
officer or his or her office ... 
Thus, where an elected official initiates the mailing, 
exercises direction or control over the content, production! or 
distribution of the document, or signs or takes credit for the 
document and the name of the elected officer or photograph appears 
in the document, it is prohibited under section 89001. 
Since the 
sheriff authors a column in the newsletter, exercises direction 
and control over the content, and will be designated as the author 
in the byline of the column, the newsletter is a prohibited mass 
mailing as defined in Regulation 18901. 
(Gilbert Advice Letter, 
No. A-89-429, copy enclosed.) 
Consequently, unless exempted from 
the restrictions of Regulation 18901 by one of the enumerated 
exceptions, the sheriff/s department may not distribute over 200 
copies of the newsletter in a calendar month unless the name of 
the sheriff and all references to the sheriff are excised from the 
newsletter. 
unsolicited Requests 
Regulation 18901(b) provides that an otherwise prohibited 
newsletter or mass mailing is exempted from the restrictions of 
section 89001 and Regulation 18901 if it contains only information 
or material sent in response to an unsolicited specific request. 
As used in this regulation, a request is unsolicited if it is not 
requested or induced by the elected officeholder or any third 
person acting at his or her behest. 
(Regulation 18901(h).) 
Thus, 
where materials are actively distributed or where individuals are 
asked to take the materials by the elected officer or any other 
person acting at the officer's behest, they are not sent in 


File No. A-89-545 
Page 4 
response to an "unsolicited" request. 
1-89-109, copyenclosed.)2/ 
(Alquist Advice Letter, No. 
Moreover, a specific request for the mailing may be oral, 
written (such as a mailing list sign-up sheet at the neighborhood 
watch meetings), or by conduct. 
For example, where members of the 
public come to a meeting or to an agency's offices and pick up 
materials for themselves, they will have made an unsolicited 
specific request for those materials. 
(Regulation 18901(h).) 
"public agency's offices" has been interpreted to include local 
county fairgrounds. 
(Leidiqh Advice Letter, No. A-89-381, copy 
enclosed. ) 
Generally, specific requests are for a single mailing. 
(Regulation 18901(h).) 
However, those persons that sign up for 
the mailing list on the sheets you have provided with your advice 
request will be making a request for continuing information on a 
subject which shall be considered a request for multiple responses 
directly related to that subject. 
In addition, if the list 
specifically states that those that sign the list will get the 
regularly published sheriff's department newsletter, the request 
shall be deemed to be a "specific request" for each issue of that 
newsletter. 
(Regulation 18901(h).) 
Consequently, the department newsletter and the mailing list 
sign-up sheet may be placed at the sheriff department's meetings 
and in booths at the local county fair. 
Individuals that pick up 
the newsletters for themselves or sign-up for the mailing list at 
these locations will have made unsolicited specific requests for 
the mailings. 
However, references by department personnel to the 
sign-up sheet or newsletters available at neighborhood watch meet-
ings or county fair would be a solicitation for a request for the 
materials. 
If you have any further questions regarding this matter, 
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
KED:JWW:plh 
Enclosures 
Sincerely, 
Kathryn E. Donovan 
General Counsel 
By: 
John W. Wallace 
Counsel, Legal Division 
Department personnel may signify that materials are available 
by displaying a sign that says "you may take one." 
Letter, No. A-89-198, copy enclosed.) 


Bernard C. Barmann 
County Counsel

Pursuant to the provisions of Government Code section 83114(b) and 
Section 18329 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations we 
are requesting, on behalf of Sheriff John R. Smith, formal written 
advice concerning his duties as Sheriff with respect to the 
provisions of Government Code section 89001 and the regulations of 
the Fair Political Practices Commission relating to mass mailings. 
Sheriff Smith's written authorization for this request is 
attached. 
Sheriff Smith's mailing address is 1350 Norris Road, 
Bakersfield, California 93308. 
The Kern County Sher iff I S 
Department has prepared a newsletter 
"Crime Prevention News" to be utilized by the Department as part 
of its neighborhood watch program. 
A copy of that newsletter is 
attached. 
It is expected that each issue of this newsletter would 
have more than 200 copies distributed each month. 
The issue on 
which we are requesting advice involves whether the methods by 
which the Department proposes to distribute the newsletter, as 
well as other information, comply with the current regulations on 
mass mailings. 
The Sheri ff' s Department periodically holds Neighborhood Watch 
meetings with Ioca 
residents 
The 
tment wau 
have the 
newsletter ava lab e at these meetings 
ividuals 
ttend 
and those interested in picking up a copy. 
There would also be at 
the table on which the newsletters were placed a sign-up sheet for 
anyone interested in being on a mailing list for the newsletter 
and other crime prevention information. 
A copy of the proposed 


Fair Political Practices Commission 
September 5, 1989 
Page 2 
sign-up sheet is enclosed and marked as "Form 1." 
There would be 
no specific attempt by Department personnel to induce attendees of 
these meetings to take the newsletter or to sign up on the mailing 
list, only an announcement that the materials were present for 
those interested. 
The Sheriff's Department also intends to have a booth at the local 
county fair. 
Copies of the newsletter and other information would 
be available at the booth for anyone interested in picking it up. 
A sign-up sheet would also be available for those interested in 
being on the mailing list for the newsletter. 
A copy of that 
proposed sign-up sheet is also enclosed as "Form 2." 
The Sher iff's Department also has various recrui tment brochures 
and informational letters it wishes to distribute to individuals 
who may be interested in becoming deputies. 
The Department hopes 
to utilize a similar means of distribution through the use of 
sign-up sheets at recrui t ing programs and at the county fair. 
Copies of the proposed sign-up sheets are attached and marked as 
"Forms 3 and 3A." 
We are requesting formal advice on whether the distribution of the 
Department's newsletter and recruiting brochures as described 
above will be consistent with the definition in Section 18901 of 
Title 2 of the Code of Regulations of an unsolicited, specific 
request for information and not prohibited by Government Code 
section 89001. 
Please do not hesitate to contact our office if you need any 
further information or have any questions. 
Yours very truly, 
B.C. BARMANN, County Counsel 
Stephen D. Schuett 
Assistant County Counsel

~KERN' COUNTY SHERIFF'S DEPARTMENT 
Crime Prevention News 
SUMMER 1989 
July brings the first issue of the Kern 
County Sheriffs Department Crime 
Prevention newsletter. 
We were 
looking for a way to keep our 
Neighborhood Watch groups up to 
date on happenings within the 
department, and also an avenue for 
watch groups within the county to 
communicate 
with 
each 
other. 
Crime Prevention News will be issued 
quarterly with all kinds of crime 
prevention tips, and information on 
Neighborhood 
Watch 
programs. 
Anyone interested in VOlunteering 
some time to assist with the next 
issue of Crime Prevention News, 
contact 
the 
Sheriffs 
Crime 
Prevention Unit at 861-7559. 
CAN WE FIND YOU?? 
Is your address readily visible day 
and nigh t for emergency service 
personnel? 
Check 
it 
today ... tomorrow may be too late! 
MESSAGE FROM SHERIFF SMITH 
Sheriff John R. Smith is a strong 
believer in the use of volunteer 
services as a complement to the paid 
staff of the Sheriffs Department. 
When asked to comment about the 
use of volunteers in the area of 
Crime 
Prevention 
he 
said, 
"Volunteers are a very important 
part of our Crime Prevention Unit. 
The dedication and generosity of 
those donating 
their 
time has 
increased 
the 
effectiveness 
and 
improved the image of the Kern 
County Sheriffs Department. Never 
has this volunteer spirit been more 
important to the community than in 
these 
times 
of 
severe 
fiscal 
constraint. 
I would like to thank 
each and every one of you for your 
support in our fight against crime." 
NEIGHBORHOOD WATCH WORKS! 
Neighborhood Watch is a well 
known crime prevention technique 
involving 
citizen 
participation. 
Neighborhood Watch was institutcO 
in 1972 by the National Sheriffs 
Association to combat the explosive 
rise in burglaries that this country 
was experiencing. 
The 
Neighborhood 
Watch 
Program operates on the concept 
that 
effective 
crime 
prevention 
requires citizen cooperation with law 
enforcement. Residential crime can 
be 
reduced 
when 
individuals, 
instructed 
by 
law 
enforcement 
agencies, take positive steps to make 
their 
property 
more 
secure. 
Neighborhood 
security 
can 
be 
improved when a majority of the citi-
zens become more vigilant and 
concerned about illegal activities in 
the community. ·Law enforcement 
officers cannot significantly impact 
crimes of opportunity such as 
residential burglary and vandalism, 
without help from citizens," says the 
Executive Director of the National 
Sheriffs Association (NSA). 
"By 
taking steps to protect their persons, 
homes and property, watching over 
that or their neighbors and reporting 
suspicious activity, citizens can help 
their 
law enforcemen t agencies 
reduce neighborhood crime. This is 
what Neighborhood Watch is all 
about." 
The thrust of NSA's crime preven-
tion activities, which date back to 
1972, is citizen involvement. 
Beyond the satisfaction with 
crime reduction, however, lies an 
important story of positive Change in 
the quality offife which accompanies 
a 
vital, 
involved 
Neighborhood 
Watch program. 
Realtors report 
that homeowners frequently prefer to 
buy a home in an area participating 
in Neighborhood Watch. 
Citizens 
speak of a renewal of com 
(See NEIGHBORHOOD WATCH, page 4) 
VOLUME I ! ISSUE I 
·WATCHERS" 
An 
important 
responsibility 
of 
·Watchers" is to report anything 
suspicious 
to 
the 
Sheriffs 
Department. 
Some of the most 
obvious things to watch for and 
report are: 
• Slow moving vehicles withoul lights 
• Parted, occupied vehicles thaI have been at 
a location for a long lime 
• Vehicles being loaded with property 
• Abandoned vehicles, may be stolen cars 
that have been dumped 
• Vehicles containing weapons 
• Anyone removing parts off of a vehicle 
• Anyone looking into parted cars, might lx' 
thinking about breaking in 
• 
Offers of merchandise at ridiculously low 
prices, could be stolen 
• Strangers entering unoccupied homes 
• Anyoneselling door-to-door in a residential 
neighborhood 
• A person running, especially if something 
valuable is being carried 
• Persons loitering around schools, parks. 
secluded areas, or in the neighborhood 
• A scream heard anywhere may mean a 
robbery or ra pe 
• 
The sound of breaking glass or other loud 
noises 
• Persons exhibiting unusual mental or 
physical symptoms, such persons may be 
injured, under the influence of drugs, or 
need medical aid 
• Continuous human traffic to and from a 
certain residence on a daily or regular 
basis, especially during late or unusual 
hours 
• 
Open or broken windows 
Remember, if the situation Changes 
while you are watching it, call the 
Sheriffs Department back and let 
them know of the changes, this may 
Change the priority level of the call. 
The non emergency phone number 
for the Communications Center is 
861-3110. 
To report suspicious 
activity, or any time a police officer 
or emergency equipment is needed, 
dial 911.

an evening. 
Don't pulI shades or 
leave notes indicating when you will 
return ... •

hours

Open or broken windows

Change the priOrity level of the call.

Stephen D. Schuett

September 25, 1989 
1415 Trustun Avenue, 15th Floor

93301 
Re: 
Letter No. 89-545 
Dear Mr. Schuett: 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act 
was received on September 20, 1989 by the Fair Political Practices 
Commission. 
If you have any questions about your advice request, 
you may contact John Wallace an attorney in the Legal Division, 
directly at (916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, or 
more information is needed, you should expect a response within 21 
working days if your request seeks formal written advice. 
If more 
information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response 
to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to 
information needed. 
If your request is for informal assistance, 
we will answer it as quickly as we can. 
(See Commission 
Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).) 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours, 
, 
Kathryn E. Donovan

sections.question:
1. 
Is a newsletter produced by the Kern County Sheriff's 
Department which contains a message from the elected sheriff a 
prohibited mass mailing under the Act? 
2. 
Are copies of the Kern County Sheriff's Department 
newsletter distributed at neighborhood watch meetings or the 
, 
~ 
All references to regulations are to Title 2, 
D 
on 
of 
Cali 
Code of 
ons. 
~0807 • (916) 322-5660  
county fair, or mailed out to persons that sign up at these loca-
tions, distributed in response to unsolicited specific requests?
sections.conclusion:
1. 
Where the sheriff authors a column in the newsletter, 
exercises direction and control over the content of the column, 
and is designated as the author in the byline of the column, there 
is sufficient direction and control over the newsletter to make it 
a prohibited mass mailing under the Act. 
2. 
The Kern County Sheriff's Department newsletter may be 
placed at the sheriff's department's, neighborhood watch meetings 
and in booths at the local county fair. 
Such mailings are 
distributed in response to an unsolicited specific request and are 
exempted from the mass mailing restrictions of the Act provided 
the request for the materials is not induced by sheriff's depart-
ment personnel. 
Similarly, copies mailed to persons that sign-up 
for the Sheriff's Department's mailing list at these locations are 
also exempted if the request for the materials is not induced by 
department personnel.
sections.facts:
The Kern County Sheriff's Department (the "department") 
produces a newsletter titled the "Crime Prevention News." 
Each 
month more than 200 copies of the newsletter are distributed, 
primarily to persons on the department's mailing list. 
Persons 
interested in receiving the newsletter and other department mail-
ings may sign up on the mailing list at the department's neighbor-
hood watch meetings. 
Printed at the top of the mailing list sign-
up sheets is the following: 
"I request the Kern County Sheriff's 
Dept. send me information regarding Crime Prevention and other 
areas of law enforcement," or similar language. 
In addition, copies of the newsletter are distributed at the 
depqrtment's neighborhood watch meetings. 
The department person-
nel that conduct the meetings set out copies of the newsletter, 
along with the mailing list sign-up sheet. 
Department personnel 
will normally make reference to the sign-up sheet and the avail-
ability of the newsletters in their presentations. 
The department is also planning to distribute newsletters and 
other material in the same manner from a booth at the local county 
fair. 
They will also provide a sign-up sheet at the booth for 
those who wish to be added to the mailing list and, additionally, 
to those persons interested in becoming deputies.
sections.analysis:
Prohibited Mass Mailings 
Section 
9001 
that no newsletter or other mass mail~ 
ing shall be sent at public expense. 
Section 82041.5 defines a  
"mass mailing" as more than 200 substantially similar pieces of 
mail sent in a calendar month. 
Regulation 18901(c) (copy 
enclosed) provides that a newsletter or other mass mailing may not 
be sent within the meaning of section 89001 if: 
(1) 
The name of the elected officer or 
his or her photograph appears on the document; 
and 
(A) 
The elected officer exercises 
direction or control over the content, 
production, or distribution of the 
document, or 
(B) 
The document is sent at the 
request or suggestion of the elected of-
ficer or his or her agent; or 
(C) 
The document is signed by, or 
is designated as being from, the elected 
officer or his or her office ... 
Thus, where an elected official initiates the mailing, 
exercises direction or control over the content, production! or 
distribution of the document, or signs or takes credit for the 
document and the name of the elected officer or photograph appears 
in the document, it is prohibited under section 89001. 
Since the 
sheriff authors a column in the newsletter, exercises direction 
and control over the content, and will be designated as the author 
in the byline of the column, the newsletter is a prohibited mass 
mailing as defined in Regulation 18901. 
(Gilbert Advice Letter, 
No. A-89-429, copy enclosed.) 
Consequently, unless exempted from 
the restrictions of Regulation 18901 by one of the enumerated 
exceptions, the sheriff/s department may not distribute over 200 
copies of the newsletter in a calendar month unless the name of 
the sheriff and all references to the sheriff are excised from the 
newsletter. 
unsolicited Requests 
Regulation 18901(b) provides that an otherwise prohibited 
newsletter or mass mailing is exempted from the restrictions of 
section 89001 and Regulation 18901 if it contains only information 
or material sent in response to an unsolicited specific request. 
As used in this regulation, a request is unsolicited if it is not 
requested or induced by the elected officeholder or any third 
person acting at his or her behest. 
(Regulation 18901(h).) 
Thus, 
where materials are actively distributed or where individuals are 
asked to take the materials by the elected officer or any other 
person acting at the officer's behest, they are not sent in  
response to an "unsolicited" request. 
1-89-109, copyenclosed.)2/ 
(Alquist Advice Letter, No. 
Moreover, a specific request for the mailing may be oral, 
written (such as a mailing list sign-up sheet at the neighborhood 
watch meetings), or by conduct. 
For example, where members of the 
public come to a meeting or to an agency's offices and pick up 
materials for themselves, they will have made an unsolicited 
specific request for those materials. 
(Regulation 18901(h).) 
"public agency's offices" has been interpreted to include local 
county fairgrounds. 
(Leidiqh Advice Letter, No. A-89-381, copy 
enclosed. ) 
Generally, specific requests are for a single mailing. 
(Regulation 18901(h).) 
However, those persons that sign up for 
the mailing list on the sheets you have provided with your advice 
request will be making a request for continuing information on a 
subject which shall be considered a request for multiple responses 
directly related to that subject. 
In addition, if the list 
specifically states that those that sign the list will get the 
regularly published sheriff's department newsletter, the request 
shall be deemed to be a "specific request" for each issue of that 
newsletter. 
(Regulation 18901(h).) 
Consequently, the department newsletter and the mailing list 
sign-up sheet may be placed at the sheriff department's meetings 
and in booths at the local county fair. 
Individuals that pick up 
the newsletters for themselves or sign-up for the mailing list at 
these locations will have made unsolicited specific requests for 
the mailings. 
However, references by department personnel to the 
sign-up sheet or newsletters available at neighborhood watch meet-
ings or county fair would be a solicitation for a request for the 
materials.

QUESTION: 1. 
Is a newsletter produced by the Kern County Sheriff's 
Department which contains a message from the elected sheriff a 
prohibited mass mailing under the Act? 
2. 
Are copies of the Kern County Sheriff's Department 
newsletter distributed at neighborhood watch meetings or the 
, 
~

D 
on 
of 
Cali 
Code of 
ons. 
~0807 • (916) 322-5660  
county fair, or mailed out to persons that sign up at these loca-
tions, distributed in response to unsolicited specific requests?

CONCLUSION: 1. 
Where the sheriff authors a column in the newsletter, 
exercises direction and control over the content of the column, 
and is designated as the author in the byline of the column, there 
is sufficient direction and control over the newsletter to make it 
a prohibited mass mailing under the Act. 
2. 
The Kern County Sheriff's Department newsletter may be 
placed at the sheriff's department's, neighborhood watch meetings 
and in booths at the local county fair. 
Such mailings are 
distributed in response to an unsolicited specific request and are 
exempted from the mass mailing restrictions of the Act provided 
the request for the materials is not induced by sheriff's depart-
ment personnel. 
Similarly, copies mailed to persons that sign-up 
for the Sheriff's Department's mailing list at these locations are 
also exempted if the request for the materials is not induced by 
department personnel.
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission stephen D. Schuett Assistant County Counsel County of Kern October 10, 1989 Administration and Courts Building 1415 Truxtun Avenue, 5th Floor Bakersfield, CA 93301 Dear Mr. Schuett: Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-89-545 This is in response to your letter requesting advice on behalf of Kern County Sheriff John R. smith concerning the ap- plication of the mass mailing provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") to the "Crime Prevention News,ii a newsletter produced and distributed by the Kern County Sheriff's Depart- ment. 1/ Please note that the Commission is in the process of revising Regulation 18901, which implements the statutory mass mailing restrictions. We anticipate noticing amendments to the regulation in October 1989, and adopting those amendments at the December 13, 1989 Commission meeting. A draft of the proposed amendments is enclosed. Nevertheless, until these amendments are formally adopted by the Commission, the current version of Regulation 18901 remains in force. Our analysis in this letter is based upon the current version of this regulation. QUESTIONS 1. Is a newsletter produced by the Kern County Sheriff's Department which contains a message from the elected sheriff a prohibited mass mailing under the Act? 2. Are copies of the Kern County Sheriff's Department newsletter distributed at neighborhood watch meetings or the 1 Government Code Section 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations section 18000, ~ All references to regulations are to Title 2, D on of Cali Code of ons. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804~0807 • (916) 322-5660 File No. A-89-545 Page 2 county fair, or mailed out to persons that sign up at these loca- tions, distributed in response to unsolicited specific requests? CONCLUSIONS 1. Where the sheriff authors a column in the newsletter, exercises direction and control over the content of the column, and is designated as the author in the byline of the column, there is sufficient direction and control over the newsletter to make it a prohibited mass mailing under the Act. 2. The Kern County Sheriff's Department newsletter may be placed at the sheriff's department's, neighborhood watch meetings and in booths at the local county fair. Such mailings are distributed in response to an unsolicited specific request and are exempted from the mass mailing restrictions of the Act provided the request for the materials is not induced by sheriff's depart- ment personnel. Similarly, copies mailed to persons that sign-up for the Sheriff's Department's mailing list at these locations are also exempted if the request for the materials is not induced by department personnel. FACTS The Kern County Sheriff's Department (the "department") produces a newsletter titled the "Crime Prevention News." Each month more than 200 copies of the newsletter are distributed, primarily to persons on the department's mailing list. Persons interested in receiving the newsletter and other department mail- ings may sign up on the mailing
